data.title: TTEC Holdings: An Opportunity For Arbitrage Specialists
data.description:
Investment thesis

The share price of TTEC Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:TTEC) jumped 52.60% on April 2, after the board of directors announced it would “engage” with Founder, Chairman, and CEO Kenneth Tuchman on his $6.85 per share proposal.

According to a company news release on April 1, a special committee of the board advised investors it would review and evaluate the unsolicited offer from Tuchman. His offer was for all TTEC shares not owned by himself or his controlled affiliates.

What are shareholders and other investors to think of the proposal and the board’s response? In this article, I review the pros and cons of various actions.

I don’t think anyone other than arbitrage specialists should buy now. On the sell side, there is a lot of uncertainty and complexity to consider.

I rate the company a Hold.

About TTEC

The company calls itself a global customer experience (CX) outsourcing partner for “marquee and high-growth brands and public sector clients.”

In its 10-K for 2024, TTEC reported that it “designs, builds, and operates technology-enabled customer experiences across live interaction channels and provides data-driven digital solutions.”

Why? To help clients “improve customer satisfaction and loyalty, increase customer revenue and profitability, and optimize overall cost to serve.”

TTEC operates through two segments: TTEC Digital and TTEC Engage. The following slide from its February 2025 Investor Overview displays what the segments do and contribute to the parent:

The company had been based in Englewood, Colorado until late 2024, when it moved to Austin, Texas. In the Q4-2024 earnings release, management said Texas had been an important part of its business for decades, and the move would put it in a business-friendly environment and more. They added the relocation would not affect their other 70+ global locations.

Why is TTEC in play?

On September 30, 2024, the board issued a news release reporting it had received “an unsolicited, preliminary, non-binding proposal letter” from Tuchman. Tuchman, who owned about 58% of the company’s common stock at that time, planned to take TTEC private. He was offering $6.85 per share, which would have been a 69.14% premium on the Friday, September 27, closing price of $4.05.

Not surprisingly, the share price jumped to $5.87 by the close on September 30. But that turned out to be a high, as the price gradually drifted down to $3.27 by April 1, 2025. As it turned out, that was just the latest decline in a slump that began in August 2021:

That long slide has lasted over three and a half years now, which tells us TTEC is an unhealthy company. To look into the issues, let’s start with a 10-year chart of revenue, net income, and basic EPS:

The company’s net income and EPS peaked in the second quarter of 2021, just before the price started its long slide. Obviously, that’s no coincidence.

And then there is that more recent, and dramatic, plunge in net income and basic EPS, which occurred in the second quarter of 2024.

In the Q2-2024 earnings report, CEO Tuchman reported, “Our results this quarter are impacted by non-cash goodwill impairment charges and related tax adjustments. We continue to operate in a dynamic macroeconomic environment where clients are facing softer demand and budget constraints putting pressure on our top line in the Engage business, while our Digital business had a solid quarter.”

Tuchman also noted into the Q4-2024 earnings report, “With our strengthened leadership alongside the actions we have taken in the second half of the year, we are on our way to healthier financial performance in 2025 and beyond.”

He did not elaborate, but it would take a significant initiative or leadership to pull the company out of this swoon that has endured for 14 consecutive quarters now.

What’s the 2025 forecast?

Its full-year 2025 outlook, posted in the Q4 earnings report, calls for revenue of $2.014 billion to $2.064 billion, which would be less than 2024’s $2.208 billion. Adjusted EBITDA is forecast to be between $215 million and $235 million, compared with 2024’s adjusted EBITDA of $202.3 million.

So, expect slightly lower revenue and slightly higher adjusted EBITDA (and likely net income) in 2025. As for EPS, the Wall Street analysts have these estimates:

These estimates use normalized EPS, which looks far healthier than basic EPS. The former was a loss of $0.21 in 2024, while the latter was a loss of $6.74.

Nevertheless, Seeking Alpha always uses normalized EPS, so that is what we will compare here. Specifically, the Wall Street analysts expect normalized EPS to flip from a loss of $0.21 to a profit of $1.06. That seems an extreme rebound, and out of sync with the company’s modest expectations for adjusted EBITDA.

The same analysts have three Up revisions and three Down revisions, which don’t align with a burst of adjusted EBITDA.

Therefore, I’m going to ignore these estimates and suggest that EPS may pick up a little, but probably not enough to become profitable.

How should TTEC shareholders react?

There may not be any happy route for existing shareholders, particularly those who bought at a higher price during sunnier days for the company.

There are two possibilities going forward. First, the board of directors accepts Tuchman’s offer, and all existing shareholders are bought out (Tuchman already owns 58% of the firm’s shares). Second, the board rejects the offer and TTEC continues to operate as it has in the past. Let’s look at each of these in more detail.

Accepting the buyout offer: This would turn TTEC into a private company and seems most likely. Members of the board, directors, likely have a reasonably close affiliation with Tuchman already. While he may not have been involved in their hiring, they have undoubtedly gotten to know each other well while working on TTEC business.

Well-intentioned directors should have minority shareholders in mind but also should consider employees and other stakeholders. Since the firm’s fundamentals have kept sliding for over three years, they may consider a radical change, such as going private, most appropriate.

By privatizing, the company will save on the many compliance and reporting requirements for public companies. In turn, that should lead to lower SG&A costs and higher net income/EPS. It would also allow management more time to turn the firm around.

Rejecting the offer: The bad news for many shareholders would be capital losses. Over the past 52 weeks, the highest price was $9.50, and over the past decade, the high was $105.16. I suspect there are still some shareholders who paid more than $6.85 for their shares, and they will face losses.

On the other hand, shareholders who took positions for less than $6.85 would find an exit that gives them an easy way out and a capital gain that depends on how much they paid to buy their stock.

Rejection also would mean sticking with a company that hasn’t been able to reverse its slumping fortunes for more than three years. As noted, the CEO sounded optimistic notes in the most recent earnings report and the Wall Street estimate for this year is rosy, but we’ve seen many, many quarters where recovery efforts have not taken hold.

As I write this analysis on April 3, 2025, the markets are trying to figure out who will be affected by newly imposed tariffs, counter-tariffs, and by how much. TTEC, which is a consulting company, is unlikely to take any direct hits. However, some of its clients may be affected and cut back on customer experience spending.

Risk arbitrage

There may be an opportunity here for arbitrage specialists. If the board accepts Tuchman’s offer, all shares will immediately be worth $6.85. The April 3 closing price was $4.45, down $0.54 or 10.82%; the magnitude of the loss was probably due to the market-wide dip that came in reaction to the new tariffs.

The difference between $4.45 and $6.85 is $2.40 or 53.93%. Why not buy now or at a similar price and collect the difference?

First, the takeover offer may not be accepted, and you would end up owning a stock that has been declining in value for more than three years.

Second, if the offer is accepted, you still would not know how long it will take to finalize, so the actual return is unknown.

Third, if accepted, your capital is tied up until the takeover is complete, and you might have earned more in some other investment than in this one.

Summing up, for anyone other than an arbitrage specialist, this would be a speculative investment with known and unknown risks.

Other thoughts on TTEC

I’m curious. The Founder, Chairman, and CEO is offering to buy all shares he and his associates do not own, at $6.85 per share. So he must see a future for the company, but has he shared that with the rest of management?

I would think that if he has a plan to transform TTEC, then that also would work for the public company. Public listing and compliance costs might make a difference to the bottom line, but I don’t see them being transformational.

On another topic, I wonder what institutional investors are thinking. They own roughly 38% of the shares not owned by Tuchman and associates, making them major partners. In the latest report, they bought and sold a roughly equal number of shares, which gives us no indication of their preferences.

Collectively, these investors own 18.25 million shares worth $81.21 million. The largest institutional holding is that of BlackRock, Inc. (BLK), which owns 1.775 million shares, worth $7.813 million.

Institutions tend to move very large blocks of shares and if they start to move one way or the other, then retail investors may be able to pick up a few clues. However, public and other non-insiders own just 1.33 million shares, so don’t expect the institutions to do much buying—there simply isn’t a suitably-sized inventory available.

TTEC Stock Valuation

I think the odds favor the board accepting Tuchman’s offer are greater than 50%, so I expect the share price will rise to $6.85, but have no idea when.

If the board rejects his offer, then there is a reasonable possibility the fundamentals will continue to slide and the price will keep falling. In that case, it’s possible it may be taken over by another firm with a plan, which would push the price up.

Given the current uncertainty, I rate TTEC a Hold.

Conclusion

Shares of TTEC shot up in the wake of news about CEO Tuchman’s offer to privatize the company. That may be good or bad news, depending on what price shareholders paid.

At the moment, I would argue it is a Buy only for arbitrage specialists because there is too much uncertainty for other investors. Those who are interested might watch and follow what institutional investors are doing.

data.title: Collect +7% From Energy Tollbooths
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

There are some businesses that demand significant upfront investment and years of development, but once in place, they become indispensable and generate steady, long-term cash flow. Their revenues are predictable and resilient, with only modest upkeep costs, allowing them to expand operations while distributing substantial profits to shareholders. One such industry is midstream energy, which serves as the critical backbone of the economy—operating the pipelines and infrastructure that keep energy moving.

It is hard to dislike the midstream business. It takes years to build (and costs several billions of dollars) and scale infrastructure, but once the network is established, it becomes vital for the country’s energy security. Companies establish long-term fee-based contracts that often insulate their earnings from commodity price swings. They are also structurally designed to distribute the bulk of their profits to shareholders. Investing in midstream is like having a tollbooth on the most important road in the city; tolls rise rapidly with inflation, and it doesn’t result in a decrease in traffic. This is the kind of business model we like to own at High Dividend Opportunities because this is the kind of investment that enriches our income stream with limited day-to-day drama. Without further ado, let’s dive into our top picks from this rewarding sector.

Pick #1: ET – Yield 6.9%

Energy Transfer LP (ET) is a midstream Master Limited Partnership that owns and operates one of the largest and most diversified portfolios of energy assets in the United States, with over 130,000 miles of pipeline and associated energy infrastructure. Energy Transfer’s strategic network spans 44 states, with assets in all of the major U.S. production basins and export terminals. Over 80 countries and territories receive exports from ET’s operations. Source

Note: ET is a Master Limited Partnership that issues a Schedule K-1 to unit holders

In addition to the direct ownership of midstream assets, ET also owns:

  • The general partner interests, the incentive distribution rights, and approximately 21% of the outstanding common units of Sunoco LP (SUN)

Sunoco is primarily engaged in energy infrastructure and the distribution of motor fuels in over 40 U.S. states, Puerto Rico, Europe, and Mexico. The company’s midstream operations include an extensive network of over 14,000 miles of pipeline and over 100 terminals, and its fuel distribution serves ~7,400 Sunoco and partner-branded locations and other commercial customers. In addition, Sunoco is the exclusive wholesale supplier of branded motor fuels, supplying to an extensive network of ~5,619 company and third-party-operated locations across the United States and Puerto Rico.

  • Lake Charles LNG Company

ET owns an LNG import terminal and regasification facility located on Louisiana’s Gulf Coast near Lake Charles, Louisiana. The import terminal has approximately 9.0 Bcf of above-ground storage capacity, and the regasification facility has a send-out capacity of 1.8 Bcf/d. Lake Charles LNG derives all of its revenue from a series of long-term contracts with a wholly owned subsidiary of Royal Dutch Shell.

  • The general partner interests and approximately 39% of the outstanding common units of USA Compression Partners LP (USAC)

USAC provides compression services to its customers, including both allowing for the processing and transportation of natural gas through the domestic pipeline system and enhancing crude oil production through artificial lift processes. These compression services play a critical role in the production, processing, and transportation of natural gas and crude oil. As of December 2024, USAC had 3.9 million horsepower in its fleet, and the company provides compression services to its customers under fixed-fee contracts with initial contract terms typically between six months to five years, where customers are required to pay a monthly fee even during periods of limited or disrupted throughput. This enhances the stability and predictability of its cash flows. Customers are mostly billed in advance of the service date, and there are annual inflation adjustments in these contracts.

ET fully and partially (through JVs) owns several other assets across the country, and describing them all is beyond the scope of today’s article.

Financials

During FY 2024, ET reported a 13% YoY growth in Adj, EBITDA, with all segments reporting strong growth, and Sunoco’s contribution stands with a 51% YoY increase due to elevated energy prices.

ET distributed $4.6 billion to unit holders in FY 2024, while generating $15.5 billion in adj. EBITDA and spending $6.1 billion towards growth and maintenance capital expenditures. The company’s $0.325/unit quarterly distribution calculates to a 6.8% yield.

ET is a More Mature Company Today

Following the pandemic-driven oil crash, ET has made several investments and acquisitions, making it a healthier and more mature company than ever before. Some of the MLP’s notable transactions include:

  • Enable Midstream Partners (December 2021): ET acquired Enable Midstream Partners for approximately $7.2 billion, adding significant natural gas gathering and processing assets to its portfolio.

  • Spindletop (March 2022): ET completed a $325 million bolt-on acquisition of Spindletop underground storage assets and ethylene storage header system.

  • Lotus Midstream (March 2023): ET purchased Lotus Midstream for around $1.4 billion, enhancing its crude oil transportation capabilities.

  • Crestwood Equity Partners LP (August 2023): This $7.1 billion acquisition expanded ET's gathering, processing, and storage operations in the natural gas, crude oil, and natural gas liquids sectors.

  • WTG Midstream Holdings LLC (July 2024): ET acquired WTG Midstream for approximately $3.25 billion, adding 6,000 miles of gas gathering pipelines and eight processing plants in the Permian Basin.

All these transactions were immediately accretive to the company’s distributable cash flows. ET’s DCF of $8.4 billion for 2024 provided a 1.8x coverage for its annual distribution. As a result, ET’s adj EBITDA experienced sustained growth, and its distribution enjoyed an impressive 21% CAGR over the past four years.

ET expects to spend $5 billion on growth capex in 2025, and projects adj. EBITDA between $16.1 and $16.5 billion (a 5.2% YoY increase at the midpoint). The company announced a long-term agreement with Cloudburst Data Centers to provide natural gas to its AI-focused data center development in Central Texas. Last month, ET placed the first 8 planned 10MW natural-gas-fired electric generation facilities into service. The company also entered a 20-year LNG Sales and Purchase Agreement with Chevron to supply 2 million tonnes per annum via its Lake Charles LNG project.

ET is self-funding its capex, and boosting its free cash flows. The company boasts 90% fee-based revenues and had its balance sheet upgraded to BBB by S&P and Fitch. ET’s leverage ratios have declined to the lower part of its 4.0 to 4.5x target range.

Insiders and independent board members own over 10% of the common units, with over 44 million units ($468 million) purchased since January 2021. The executive chairman, Mr. Kelcy Warren has notably purchased 61 million units ($675 million) since January 2019. The company’s co-CEOs hold 6x their annual base salary in ET units, overall indicating a strong alignment between management’s strategy and objectives and unit holder returns.

Pick #2: EIPI – Yield 7.3%

Perhaps the only notable drawback of investing in MLPs is the dreaded Schedule K-1, which tends to bring undesired complications in the tax filing process. While many see the benefits of tax-deferred distributions, some find the hassle to be unworthy of the pursuit. There are also international investors who experience highly unfavorable tax withholding by investing in Master Limited Partnerships.

FT Energy Income Partners Enhanced Income ETF (EIPI) is a diversified fund with 102 holdings and $962.3 million in net assets. EIPI is deeply diversified in the energy industry, with significant exposure to midstream leaders like EPD, ET, OneOK Inc. (OKE), MPLX, Kinder Morgan (KMI), Enbridge (ENB), etc. Source

About 90% of EIPI’s assets are invested in oil and gas and utility firms, providing solid exposure to not just MLPs but the broader fee- and infrastructure-heavy midstream and utility business, with relatively stable earnings despite economic volatility.

EIPI was formed in May 2024 through the merger and conversion of CEFs FEN and FEI. Since it began trading in its new structure as an ETF, EIPI has delivered solid total returns, beating that of the S&P 500, and the energy and utility indices.

This fund boosts returns by selling covered call options on securities it owns, earning extra income but capping some upside. The strategy balances dividends, call premiums, and growth potential to optimize returns. Notably, the average time until the expiration of calls stood at 38 days, indicating a relatively short-term options strategy. The fund sells Call-Out-Of-The-Money options with strike prices ~8.29% higher than current market prices, which makes them less likely to be exercised unless the equity prices rise rapidly (which is a bit rare in the boring midstream business). It is also important to note that ~38% of EIPI’s portfolio is tied to written covered calls, indicating over a third of the holdings are used to enhance returns through option premiums.

EIPI pays monthly distributions of $0.125/month, reflecting a 7.3% annualized yield, significantly exceeding the yield of its top two holdings, EPD and ET. Notably, the ETF paid a massive special payment in December to distribute short-term capital gains realized from its active portfolio management.

EIPI presents a solid choice to obtain diversified exposure to midstream energy firms, collect great monthly income, and avoid the pesky Schedule K-1!

Conclusion

Income-focused investors, particularly those looking toward retirement, must seek out dividends without drama. Midstream energy companies offer this compelling opportunity as these businesses are built to endure through various economic cycles and political climates, providing stable, predictable cash flow and a reliable dividend stream. By focusing on long-term, fee-based contracts and minimizing exposure to volatile commodity prices, midstream companies provide much-needed income stability in retirement. This type of investment is exactly what many retirees and income-seeking investors should aspire to, a secure, inflation-resistant cash flow that allows for peace of mind as you navigate the complexities of your financial future.

This article discusses two picks, Energy Transfer and EIPI, a midstream energy focused ETF. The former provides the benefits of deferred tax distributions, while the latter provides investors an option to benefit from this cash-flow-rich sector without the complexities of a Schedule K-1. Investing is all about making choices that align with your personal financial goals, and when it comes to income investing, there's always an option tailored to fit every investor's needs. In the ever-changing landscape, it's businesses like these that can help expand your income while providing the stability you need to meet your financial goals. This is the beauty of our Income Method, and the power of income investing.

data.title: BWX Technologies: A Strong Nuclear Bet, But With Little Margin For Error
data.description:
Investment Thesis

BWX Technologies, Inc. (BWXT) is a major player in the energy field, where federal projects drive about 80% of its income. That income is getting a boost from smart acquisitions—like Aerojet Ordnance Tennessee and Kinectrics—that give the company a stronger foothold in specialty materials and nuclear medicine. Those deals have put BWXT on a growth trajectory.

The company has secured some high-profile million-dollar defense contracts and just opened an innovation center in Virginia where it's developing fresh nuclear technologies for use on land, at sea and even in space.

While increased investments and changes to its project mix have dented profitability, BWXT still managed a 3% revenue increase by the end of 2024. It's optimistic about strong growth ahead—especially in its commercial division. But that growth could be hindered by a recent rise in debt.

Although BWXT remains profitable, it needs to get more efficient and turn more revenue into cash flow. Even with a strong business and promising growth prospects, I consider that BWXT's shares look expensive compared to both the industry and its own history. If those valuations are any guide, there's not much room for error.

That makes it a good idea for long-term investors who already own the stock to hold with it. For those considering getting in, though waiting for a market correction or a better entry point may be wise.

Overview

BWXT is a leading provider of nuclear components and fuel to the U.S. government and commercial sector. Its capabilities range from supplying nuclear propulsion for NASA projects to powering U.S. Navy submarines and aircraft carriers. It also provides energy to homes and businesses and supplies materials to medical facilities using nuclear technology.

Though the industry includes several branches, it generates 80% of its revenue from contracts with the U.S. government. And so, the defense sector's projections for the years ahead are critical to consider. As I shared in my analysis of Woodward, Inc. (WWD):

"The U.S. aerospace and defense market is projected to grow at an annual rate of 2.4% until 2028, focused on investments in defense and upward demand for both commercial and military aerospace products."

In its plan to stay at the front of new ideas, BWXT has done big buys and deals in the first months of 2025. On January 3, it finished the acquisition of Aerojet Ordnance Tennessee, Inc. (A.O.T), strengthening its special materials work in the government area with key items like used uranium and tungsten. ͏ Days later, on January 7, it announced it was buying Kinectrics for $525 million, growing its role in the nuclear and medicine isotope areas through reactor life services and medical isotope making. Then, on January 27, BWXT got deals worth more than C$1 billion to make the Pickering nuclear plant last longer and build a small modular reactor in Darlington, Ontario, boosting the Canadian nuclear field.

Also, on February 19, it announced the concession of $2.1 billion in deals to make naval nuclear reactor parts for submarines and aircraft carriers, with work going until 2030. To match its growth into new technologies, on March 26, it opened its Innovation Campus in Lynchburg, Virginia, dedicated to the work of nuclear tech for land, sea, and space uses. With more than 350 workers on projects for NASA, the Department of Energy and the Department of Defense and a $65 million investment it pushes big projects like Project Pele mobile microreactor and the making of nuclear thermal drive for space travel which puts its position as a main player in energy safety and tech innovation

4Q 2024

According to company data, Q4 2024 ended with a 3% increase in revenue (from $726 MM to $746 MM), driven by the very good performance of Commercial Operations, although partially offset by a slight decline in the Government Operations segment.

However, the adjustment in the project mix and the higher level of investments had a negative effect on profitability: adjusted EBITDA was $130 MM, 12% below Q4 2023. Non-GAAP EPS also contracted by 9%.

Within Government Operations, revenues reached $595 MM, 1% below the previous year, given the lower purchase of long-term materials and a decrease in the processing of special materials, despite the increase in the manufacturing of naval components and microreactor activities.

Meanwhile, Commercial Operations had 23% growth in revenues, and revenues reached $152 MM in Q4 2024, driven by strong demand in the medical segment and a higher volume of commercial nuclear components and fuels.

Outlook

According to BWXT's estimates, it expects solid growth in both segments, with Government Operations increasing at a mid-single-digit pace, driven by its activities and the recent acquisition of A.O.T., while for Commercial Operations they project an increase of around 50%, with a double-digit increase, adding the contribution of Kinectrics

At the level of profitability, the company projects adjusted EBITDA margins of approximately 20% in Government Operations and between 14% and 15% in Commercial Operations.

On the other hand, despite the increase in debt, which will increase interest expense between $10 MM and $12 MM, it expects to maintain solid free cash flow, with a capex rate of between 5% and 6% of sales.

In terms of net profitability, it foresees a slight increase in the tax rate due to the change in the geographical composition of income, while planned share buybacks will offset the dilution, keeping the number of shares outstanding stable.

Valuation

Analyzing aspects such as growth, profitability and efficiency, I created a combining profile for the stock. Nonetheless, the profile in its current state poses limitations to the firm’s long-term attractiveness.

I will begin with the company’s profitability. It is noteworthy to mention that the company holds a couple of strongest segments: its net margin is 10.4%, higher than the industry’s 6.2%, meaning the company’s profit conversion from income is more efficient than others.

Likewise, given that ROE is 28 percent, with BWXT outperforming the company’s 12.7 percent industry, it indicates that the company is wisely using capital from investors– clearly BWXT is effectively employing investment funds. However, assessing ROTC and ROA data seems to indicate that while the business is profitable, its operating efficiency relative to the average over the last five years is concerning.

Moreover, with EPS estimating a growth from 5.3% to 30.7% between 2025 and 2028, the results indicate a promising outlook. Although, in my assessment, the market has already integrated the expectations into the property’s value based on multi criteria analysis with other indicators.

Here’s where I see some red flags about BWXT, given that the multiples seem too high both in the company’s history as well as against the sector. The P/E [FWD] is 28.9x, higher than the sector’s 19.1x which suggests a premium of 45.1%. Other indicators which are not favorable for valuation include the EV/EBITDA ratio of 18.3x versus 10.5x for the sector, EV/EBIT of 22.5x compared to 15.1x for the sector, and P/B of 7.05x against 2.9x in the sector. It is my opinion that these ratios do not allow much margin for strategic execution success from the company.

In the same fashion, the P/S is elevated both in TTM and FWD at 3.4x and 3.05x respectively compared to the sector’s 1.4x and 1.36x. From what has been said, indicates that there is a proportionate decline in return for investment on revenue generation. To further validate the point, the PEG GAAP and Non-GAAP (2.25x and 2.43x) clearly show the stock is not cheap.

In analyzing the cash flow, I attempted to gain a medium to long-term perspective on how the entity has the potential to finance growth. Although it is better than the last five-year average by 33.2%, its free cash flow margin of 4.1% is lower than the industry average of 6.8%. This may signal that the company might face some challenges in meeting its growth targets without incurring additional debt or equity financing.

Risks

The industry in which the company operates is highly regulated. So, changes in the regulations could restrict business expansion due to increased costs. It is also exposed to penalties for failing to comply, not to mention the usual damage to reputation.

While there is a global change towards cleaner sources of energy, and a favorable attitude to nuclear energy, it has to be recognized that there are several other competitors in the renewable energy segment. The term “nuclear” usually raises some safety alarms. The perception along with heightened competition in the use of an alternative energy source could have unfavorable impacts on demand.

Continuing with competition issues, there is strong growth in investment in the sector from China and Russia, which could reduce the US market share, and therefore, that of BWXT. One of the main challenges I see is in the expansion into the civilian market, since the company must compete with already established firms.

Another point I consider important is the company's strong dependence on government contracts. According to the figures for the last two years, these represented 80% of the company's income. This may be considered a strength since it provides a source of income stability it also opens the business to potential defense contract cancellations or cutbacks.

Finally, BWXT has a very high valuation, above its industry. Moreover, in an industry where ongoing innovation is vital, its cash flow is weak and its profit margin is narrow.

Conclusion

Based on what has been analyzed, I conclude that BWXT is a company with solid fundamentals in terms of profitability and growth prospects. I consider that its current valuation is high compared to its history and the sector. While EPS projections are optimistic, the market already priced in accelerated growth, leaving little room for negative surprises.

Therefore, for the long term, the company could justify its valuation if it managed to expand its margins and improve its conversion of revenue to cash flow. However, in my view, for investors with a focus on value, the stock looks expensive at the current price. It is necessary to monitor the performance of the coming months in terms of price.

For those long-term investors who have the asset in their portfolio, I recommend to hold it, while for those who wish to enter, I estimate that a more attractive entry point could occur in a market correction or in a reduction of its valuation multiples to levels more aligned with its historical average.

data.title: 3 Steps To Retiring With $1 Million In High-Yield Stocks
data.description:
Co-produced by Austin Rogers for High Yield Investor

Retirement naturally comes with mixed emotions. It is the end of an era, but also the beginning of a new one.

In the midst of such a big life change, it is helpful to have as much certainty and peace of mind about one's nest egg as possible.

The standard advice is to follow the “4% rule”—withdraw 4% of your portfolio value per year, every year. But there are downsides to this strategy. While it works well during bull markets when stocks are rising much more than 4% per year, it can be destructive to one's portfolio value during recessions and bull markets when retirees need to sell beaten-down stocks to fund living expenses.

Another approach is to invest one's nest egg into higher-yielding yet fundamentally solid stocks and live entirely from the dividend income, leaving the principal untouched.

If you have $1 million saved and are attracted to this approach, or if you are somewhere along in the process of accumulating $1 million and want to eventually retire this way, here are three simple steps to follow.

Step 1: Plan

First and foremost, you need to determine how much annual income you want or need.

This depends on many factors.

  • Have you completely paid off your mortgage?
  • Are you in good health? It's always a good idea to assume that no matter your age, your healthcare spending is probably only going up from here.
  • Do you expect any major spending items (such as a new car or family member's college tuition) in the coming years?
  • Do you have any other sources of income, such as Social Security, a pension, rental properties, part-time work, etc.?

One retiree's passive income needs may be very different than another's. One may need only $40,000 for a comfortable retirement, while another may need $80,000.

Step 2: Build

The goal of the entire portfolio of one's investable assets is maximal yet stable income generation.

To minimize the risk of needing to sell stock to pay for living expenses, we recommend maintaining a healthy cash cushion. Financial advisors typically suggest retirees keep at least one year's worth of living expenses in cash. The purpose of this cash cushion is to (1) cover any big, unexpected expenses, and to (2) fill the gap with lower income from dividend cuts during recessions.

Fortunately for income investors in today's environment, though, holding cash equivalents does not entirely take that capital out of the game. Many cash equivalent funds offer relatively generous yields. Here are some examples along with their current 30-day SEC yields:

  • iShares 0-3 Month Treasury ETF (SGOV) - 4.2%
  • iShares Ultra Short-Term Bond Active ETF (ICSH) - 4.6%
  • JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST) - 4.5%
  • Vanguard Ultra-Short Bond ETF (VUSB) - 4.7%
  • Vanguard Ultra-Short Treasury ETF (VGUS) - 4.2%

Of course, the Fed is expected to cut their key policy rate at least 2–3 times this year, which will cause these cash equivalent ETFs' yields to decline. But investors can probably expect 3% or higher yields from them for the foreseeable future.

Once the cash cushion is in place, it's time to build out the rest of the investment portfolio.

As we explained in “Why Retirees Don't Need A Bond Allocation,” we think retirees would do well to forgo bonds and bond funds (aside from the cash cushion) and focus on stocks. While bonds offer income stability during recessions, they also provide zero income growth, leaving coupon income vulnerable to the slow erosion of inflation.

Retirees need to maintain their real purchasing power not just for a year or two but for multiple decades. Bonds can only help with that if they generate excess income (beyond what's necessary to fund living expenses) that can be reinvested into more income-generating assets.

We would start by establishing a solid and diversified base of ETFs that provide either reliable dividend growth or high yields.

Here are some options we would gravitate toward:

ETF Dividend Yield Estimated Dividend Growth Rate
Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) 3.6% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV) 3.5% Mid-Single-Digit
Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX) 4.4% Mid-Single-Digit
JP Morgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) 7.5% Flat
Virtus InfraCap US Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA) 9.4% Flat
VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) 10.8% Flat

This is basically a barbell approach, with the first three ETFs holding a diversified set of dividend growth stocks and the latter three ETFs employing various high-yielding strategies.

SCHD is often considered the “gold standard” dividend ETF for its history of generating double-digit dividend growth combined with an above-average dividend yield.

HDV follows a very similar moderately high-yielding strategy but with more emphasis on energy companies and defensive sectors like consumer staples and utilities.

MLPX owns a small basket of high-quality midstream oil & gas companies that own pipelines, storage facilities, and processing plants.

JEPI employs a unique options-writing strategy on top of an actively managed portfolio of dividend-paying stocks to generate options premiums on top of the portfolio's dividends.

PFFA is an actively managed fund that invests in preferred equities, which are a hybrid between common stocks and bonds. The ETF's active managers have generated the best total returns of any preferred ETF.

Finally, BIZD owns a basket of business development companies ("BDCs"), which are non-bank lenders that make loans to private, middle-market businesses. We find BDCs to be a good way for the average individual investor to gain exposure to the growing private credit industry.

Beyond the base of ETFs to provide stability and diversification, we would then enhance the portfolio with a group of individual dividend stocks.

The key with individual stocks is to balance yield with dividend safety. Many income investors make the mistake of buying ultra-high-yielding stocks that ultimately prove to be value traps with unsustainable dividends.

A good place to start looking is among dividend aristocrats, contenders, and challengers.

  • Dividend Aristocrats: 25+ consecutive years of dividend growth
  • Dividend Contenders: 10–24 years of consecutive dividend growth
  • Dividend Challengers: 5–9 years of consecutive dividend growth

There are multiple lists on the Internet of such stocks, one of which can be found here.

We would also own at least a handful of real estate investment trusts ("REITs") (VNQ), focusing again on dividend growth track record, payout ratio, balance sheet strength, and portfolio quality.

Some blue-chips in the REIT space we would gravitate toward include:

  • Agree Realty (ADC) - best-in-class single-tenant net lease retail properties
  • Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) - top-tier life science campuses
  • Sila Realty Trust (SILA) - single-tenant medical buildings
  • VICI Properties (VICI) - Las Vegas & regional casino resorts
  • W.P. Carey (WPC) - single-tenant industrial and logistics properties

The blend of ETFs, dividend growth stocks, and REITs will depend a lot on the individual investor's risk tolerance, income needs, and sphere of competence.

Step 3: Maintain

There is both an art and a science to maintaining an investment portfolio once it is built.

It starts with the frequency with which one checks their portfolio performance. Too frequently checking one's account often leads to over-trading, triggering lots of taxable transactions without adding much value. But too infrequently checking one's portfolio can lead to greater losses in cases where a company's financials slowly erode and increase the likelihood of a dividend cut.

Checking one's portfolio around once a week strikes us as a pretty good balance.

Of course, portfolio maintenance means more than just checking ticker prices. In fact, ticker prices can be misleading by diverging from the fundamentals for a while. What should matter most to investors are the fundamentals: revenue, earnings, free cash flow, dividend coverage, how management are allocating capital, and so on.

It pays for a DIY retiree to devote the time to become proficient in business fundamentals, at least for their own holdings.

Fortunately, there are many tools available to help investors with that nowadays, including websites like Seeking Alpha, AI-generated summaries of earnings reports and conference calls, and investor presentations on public companies' investor relations websites.

By maintaining a balanced portfolio, selectively adding to solid holdings in the midst of pullbacks, and only selling stocks when they are at elevated risk of dividend cuts, retirees can efficiently manage their nest eggs without letting it take over their lives.

Bottom Line

Retiring on your own, self-managed nest egg of $1 million is by no means easy or carefree, but it is doable. Most people end up giving their money to financial advisors, which is a perfectly reasonable approach. But one can save a small fortune in advisory fees by managing their nest egg themselves.

By following the three simple steps above, investors can put themselves in a very strong position to comfortably retire with both peace of mind and the knowledge that their principal is remaining intact.

Of course, we here at High Yield Investor make it our life's work to help individual investors achieve their dream retirement. Our Retirement Portfolio is intended to do just that, and we are very pleased with its results so far.

data.title: Diageo: Better Than Expected Tariff Hit Meets A 50% Drawdown
data.description:
Diageo (NYSE:DEO) shares were in a 45% drawdown before Trump was elected, primarily due to lower alcohol consumption worldwide, declining sales, and shrinking margins. Trump's tariffs exacerbated the selloff, and shares are now trading 50% below all-time highs.

However, Trump's 'Liberation Day' tariffs were not as bad as expected for the company.

With the stock trading around its 10-year lows and back at 2012 levels, it's time to revisit our bearish stance.

Let's dive in.

Introduction To Diageo

Diageo is one of the largest alcoholic beverage sellers in the world, with over $20 billion in annual sales. The British company sells every type of spirit you can think of, with global brands like Don Julio, Captain Morgan, Guinness, Johnnie Walker, Smirnoff, and others.

I've been covering the company on Seeking Alpha since 2023, mostly with a bearish view, given the company's lack of growth prospects and relatively high valuation.

Getting To Know The Industry

The alcohol industry has two major end-point customers. One is the at-home drinker, and one is the out-of-home drinker. The majority of sales include a middle-men, and Diageo has effectively no direct-to-consumer relationships.

The strength of the industry is highly reliant on good inventory management from all players. During Covid, Diageo and its peers got a major boost, as people were buying alcohol for their homes. Think about it, instead of one gin bottle serving the entire customer base of a local bar for a few days, all these customers bought one bottle each, resulting in faster depletions and replenishment cycles.

Also, alcohol consumption was high because people were working from home, and didn't worry as much about being hungover at work in the following day.

These trends pushed Diageo shares to an all-time high at $220 right at the end of 2021 and the last of the Covid lockdowns.

Diageo Had Enough Problems Even Without Tariffs

After achieving the 2021 highs, restaurants and bar were back open, and people started going back outside, whether it was work, travel, or just going out. The industry was caught off-guard with huge inventories, as it planned for continued strength, like it saw in the Covid years.

Meanwhile, many celebrities and influencers created their own alcohol brands during Covid, taking market share from the large players. In addition, it turns out, people aren't drinking as much as they used to, especially Gen Z.

Diageo sales have been essentially flat since 2022. In the half that ended in December (the company's fiscal H1-25), organic growth was 1%, driven by price and mix, though net sales dropped by 1%.

Meanwhile, overhead and strategic investments are dragging down margins, with operating profit down 4.9% Y/Y, or down 1.2% organically. EPS dropped even further, by about 10%, and net leverage increased to 3.1x adjusted EBITDA.

As you can see, Diageo and the entire industry are pressured, which has led the company to cut its medium-term guidance.

The problems have started even before any tariff-related headwinds, and then, they arrived.

Better Than Expected Tariff Hit

From Diageo's earnings release:

On Saturday 1 February 2025, President Trump in one of three separate Executive Orders announced the implementation of 25% tariffs on goods imported into the US from Canada and Mexico. These tariffs, applicable from 4 February 2025, would apply to our products made in Canada and Mexico in transit after midnight 1 February 2025.

In the US, our largest market, the products which would be impacted by the tariffs would mainly be our tequila portfolio, which given geographic origin requirements must be made in Mexico, and also Canadian whisky.

Before Trump's 'Liberation Day' tariffs, the market was worried about additional tariffs on Mexico and Canada, as well as higher tariffs on other countries where Diageo produces.

Now, it appears about 55% of Diageo's sales will be completely exempt from tariffs, and that only about 2% of the company's profits are expected to be hit.

When it comes to measuring the tariff threat priced into the stock, my estimate is that it was about a 9% hit:

On one hand, Diageo performed better than the S&P 500, so you can argue no specific hit. However, consumer staples have been outperforming the market by 15%, and Brown-Forman (BF.B), an alcoholic beverage seller that does most of its production in the US, has been flat.

This makes me think that if it weren't for tariffs, Diageo would probably be flat as well.

Valuation

So, the tariff threat is not as bad as the market thought. However, does that, by itself, make Diageo an attractive investment? I'm sceptical.

At $108 a share, Diageo is trading at 17 times forward earnings, and 16 times '26 estimates, with expectations for low-single digit revenue growth at best in the foreseeable future.

This places Diageo right in line with other beverage peers, all of which are expected to generate better growth. And, the structural headwinds of the alcohol industry remain.

To me, this reflects that Diageo is hovering around fair valuation.

Conclusion

Diageo has been losing market share in the pressured alcohol industry since 2021. The company's sales have been flat, and margins have declined.

These problems are structural, and made me a bear even before tariffs.

Now, although the tariff threat turned out to be better than expected (for now), I don't see a reason to be excited about the stock. Growth limitations persist, and valuation isn't too attractive either.

In my view, Diageo can be a near-term low-risk play for a volatile market, but I'd much rather look for opportunities elsewhere amid the recent sharp market selloff.

Therefore, I reiterate Diageo as a 'Hold'.

data.title: Dell: An Undervalued AI Play
data.description:
Dell Technologies (DELL) is executing well in the server market and reporting strong growth in its Infrastructure Solutions Group as a result. With spending on AI products only set to ramp up, Dell is well-positioned to capitalize on growing spending, especially on servers and networking products, and I see a strong upside revaluation case for Dell in the years ahead. Dell’s Infrastructure Solutions Group has also emerged as a major catalyst for an operating income uplift, which could push shares of the hardware company into a new up-leg. Since shares of Dell are only trading at 9.0X forward earnings, I believe the hardware company is undervalued and could be an attractive play on both the AI and server markets.

Previous rating

I called Dell a "Deep Value AI Stock" in September 2024 due to the company’s exposure to the Data Center market and AI, and its relatively low valuation based off of earnings. What remains highly attractive about Dell is that the company has a growing server business which I expect to benefit handsomely from higher, and potentially accelerating, investments in the Data Center market. With Dell trading at a relatively low 9X P/E ratio, I believe the hardware company is looking at a multi-year run-way of high EPS and cash flow growth.

Growth and revaluation potential driven by growing server demand in Dell's ISG

Dell managed to turn its business around and achieve positive top-line growth in FY 2025 due to massive investments in AI-optimized server products. Dell reported 95.6B in total revenue for FY 2025, showing 8% growth and a 22 PP swing in the top-line growth rate last year, mainly due to escalating demand for AI-optimized servers and other networking products. In fact, Dell's server and networking business, which is grouped together in the Infrastructure Solutions Group, is seeing exceptional strong growth and the segment has emerged as a catalyst for an operating income surge.

Therefore, the Infrastructure Solutions Group is the core element of my bullish outlook for the hardware company as well.

As I indicated in my last work on the hardware company, advancements in AI are pushing the entire server market into a new upgrade cycle, which is where Dell has a lever to grow its earnings.

In the fourth-quarter, Dell’s Infrastructure Solutions Group grew its top line 22% Y/Y to $11.4B, but its servers and networking products experienced a significantly more powerful revenue boost: they grew 37% year-over-year while the segment’s operating income surged twice as fast as its segment top line (+44%).

Infrastructure Solutions Group is currently lifting up Dell's entire business and with AI investments expected to grow fast in the future, ISG will remain instrumental in helping Dell grow both its top line and operating income. In fact, ISG has become so significant for Dell's business represented 46% of Dell's FY 2025 revenue and 65% of total non-GAAP operating income.
